questions.question: A file has permissions `-rw-r-----`. Owner is `root`, group is `www-data`. User `deploy` is a member of `www-data`. Can `deploy` write to this file?

options.text: Yes — deploy is in the www-data group, and group permissions include write (rw-). Group members can read and write.
No — only the owner (root) has write permission. Group permissions are r-- (read only). deploy would need sudo.
Yes — any user in the www-data group inherits the owner's permissions, so deploy gets rw- access just like root.
No — the file has no execute permission, which is required before write operations can succeed on Linux.

questions.explanation: Permissions: rw- (owner), r-- (group), --- (others). Group has read only.

questions.question: A service isn't starting after reboot. `systemctl status myapp` shows 'inactive (dead)'. Most likely reason?

options.text: The service crashed. Systemd permanently disabled it to prevent restart loops.
The unit file exists but `systemctl enable` was never run. Systemd only auto-starts enabled services at boot.
Server ran out of memory during boot. Systemd kills non-essential services when memory is low.
The service binary was deleted. Systemd detects missing binaries at boot and marks the service dead.

questions.explanation: systemctl enable creates boot symlink. Without it, service exists but won't auto-start.

questions.question: Root partition at 100%. You `rm` a 5GB log file, but `df -h` still shows 100%. Why?

options.text: rm only removes the directory entry. If a process still has the file open, disk space isn't freed until the file handle is released.
Linux caches deleted files in a recycle bin for 24 hours. Space freed after cache expires or running sync.
rm needs root to actually free space. Without sudo, it only removes the filename but preserves data.
The file was hardlinked elsewhere. rm only decrements link count; data persists until all hardlinks removed.

questions.explanation: Open file descriptors prevent space reclamation. Use lsof to find the holding process.

questions.question: Which approach most directly finds what process is listening on port 8080?

options.text: ps aux | grep 8080 — searches all processes for port 8080 in their arguments.
ss -tlnp | grep 8080 — shows TCP listening sockets with process ID and name per port.
lsof /var/run/*.8080 — checks Unix socket files associated with port 8080.
cat /etc/services | grep 8080 — maps port numbers to service names.

questions.explanation: ss -tlnp shows listening TCP sockets with process info. Direct and reliable.

questions.question: Process uses 80% RAM. You want OTHER processes to get CPU preference. Correct approach?

options.text: renice +10 -p PID — increases nice value, giving lower CPU priority. Other processes get more CPU time.
kill -SIGSTOP PID — freezes process, releasing memory pages to others while keeping it alive.
ulimit -m 50 — sets max resident memory to 50%, forcing kernel to swap excess.
cgroups memory.limit to cap at 50%. OOM killer reclaims excess and redistributes.

questions.explanation: Question asks about CPU preference, not memory. renice adjusts CPU scheduling priority.

questions.question: https://example.com shows 'connection refused' but http works. What does 'connection refused' tell you?

options.text: SSL certificate is invalid. Server refused TLS handshake due to authentication failure.
Nothing is listening on port 443. Server's TCP stack rejected the connection — no process bound to that port.
DNS misconfiguration routing HTTPS to a different server without the application.
Browser HSTS policy blocking the connection. Site previously flagged as insecure.

questions.explanation: Connection refused is TCP-level: SYN reached server but no process listening on port 443.

questions.question: App behind Nginx reverse proxy. Logs show proxy IP instead of user IPs. Fix?

options.text: Configure Nginx to add X-Forwarded-For header. App reads client IP from that header instead of connection IP.
Remove the reverse proxy. Proxies always mask client IP with no workaround.
Enable transparent proxy mode in Nginx to forward TCP packets with original source IP.
App reads Host header for client IP. Nginx sets Host to original client's IP address.

questions.explanation: X-Forwarded-For is the standard mechanism for preserving client IP through proxies.

questions.question: Can ping 8.8.8.8 but `ping google.com` fails with 'Name or service not known'. Issue?

options.text: Network interface only allows IP connections. Domain names need separate DNS interface.
DNS resolution broken. Server reaches internet (IP works) but can't resolve domains. Check /etc/resolv.conf.
Firewall blocks HTTP/HTTPS. Ping uses ICMP bypassing firewall, but DNS uses HTTP which is blocked.
Google DNS (8.8.8.8) responds to ICMP but blocks DNS queries from your IP range.

questions.explanation: IP works = connectivity fine. Domain fails = DNS broken. Check /etc/resolv.conf for nameserver entries.

questions.question: App behind ALB: works at low traffic, 502 errors under load. Health checks pass. Why?

options.text: ALB connection limit reached. Hard cap of 10K concurrent connections.
Backends responding too slowly under load. ALB timeout expires before response, returns 502.
ALB SSL certificate can't handle throughput. TLS handshakes become bottleneck.
AWS rate-limits security group rules under high traffic, throttling ALB-to-backend connections.

questions.explanation: 502 from ALB = backend invalid response or timeout. Under load, backends slow past ALB timeout.

questions.question: Committed and pushed a secret to GitHub. Why won't `git revert` + push make it safe?

options.text: git revert only works for merge commits. Need git reset --hard + force push.
Old commit with key still exists in Git history. Anyone can browse old commits. Must rewrite history AND rotate key.
GitHub caches reverted commits for 90 days on CDN.
git revert creates syntax error in commit chain. Push rejected by verification.

questions.explanation: Git history preserves everything. Bots scan repos in seconds. Always rotate compromised credentials.

questions.question: Two devs work on different files, push to same branch. Dev B's push rejected. Why?

options.text: Git locks branches during push. Dev B must wait for 5-minute lock expiry.
Dev B's local branch behind remote. Git rejects non-fast-forward pushes. Must pull first.
Git detected potential merge conflict. Blocks all pushes until resolved on server.
Branch protection blocks concurrent pushes within short time window.

questions.explanation: Git requires fast-forward pushes. Remote has Dev A's commits that B doesn't have. Pull first.

questions.question: Broken feature branch (50 commits) merged to main. Others pushed work after. Safest undo?

options.text: git revert -m 1 <merge-SHA> — single new commit undoing entire merge, preserves subsequent work.
git reset --hard to before merge + force push. Cleanest approach, won't affect later commits.
Cherry-pick all post-merge commits to new branch, delete and recreate main.
git bisect to find breaking commit, revert just that one to preserve useful work.

questions.explanation: git revert -m 1 on merge commit creates reversal commit preserving all history. B destroys later work.

questions.question: Script works with `bash script.sh` but 'Permission denied' with `./script.sh`. Why?

options.text: ./ syntax only works for compiled binaries, not interpreted scripts.
File lacks execute permission. ./script.sh needs execute bit (chmod +x). bash script.sh only needs read.
Current directory . not in PATH. Shell can't find script without PATH entry.
Script has syntax error caught differently in ./ mode which uses stricter parsing.

questions.explanation: ./file executes directly (needs +x). bash file passes as argument (needs read only).

questions.question: Bash script works on Mac, garbled output on Linux. File created on Windows. What's happening?

options.text: Windows UTF-16 encoding vs Linux UTF-8 corrupts multi-byte characters.
Windows CRLF line endings (\r\n) vs Linux LF (\n). Extra \r treated as data, corrupting output.
Different Bash versions with incompatible syntax between Mac and Linux.
macOS Bash is Zsh-based with different file I/O. Script worked by accident on Mac.

questions.explanation: Windows CRLF adds invisible \r to every line, breaking string operations on Linux.

questions.question: Script has `set -e`. Exits unexpectedly at `grep -q "error" logfile.log`. File exists, no match. Why?

options.text: set -e exits on any non-zero exit code. grep returns 1 when pattern not found, triggering exit.
set -e treats grep specially. Zero matches is fatal in strict mode.
-q flag suppresses output AND error handling. Without -q it would handle 'not found' correctly.
set -e pre-validates file operations. Detects pattern doesn't exist and exits to prevent false negatives.

questions.explanation: set -e exits on non-zero return. grep returns 1 for no match. Fix: grep ... || true

questions.question: Python script: 500 API calls, 1 sec each, 8 min total. Best way to speed up?

options.text: multiprocessing.Pool with 500 workers. Each call gets own OS process for true parallelism on CPU-bound work.
asyncio + aiohttp. API calls are I/O-bound. Async handles hundreds of concurrent requests in single thread.
threading with GIL disabled. Python 3.13+ GIL-free mode required for threads to make simultaneous calls.
subprocess.Popen for 500 separate Python processes. OS scheduler handles concurrency.

questions.explanation: API calls are I/O-bound, not CPU-bound. asyncio is designed for concurrent I/O without thread/process overhead.

questions.question: EC2 in public subnet, public IP, SG allows SSH from 0.0.0.0/0. Can't SSH. What to check?

options.text: Instance IAM role needs ec2:SSHAccess policy to accept connections.
Network ACL (NACL) might block port 22. NACLs are stateless, evaluated before security groups.
Instance in maintenance window. AWS blocks SSH during security patching.
Public IP expired. AWS recycles IPs every 24 hours without Elastic IP.

questions.explanation: NACLs are stateless, evaluated before SGs. Need explicit inbound AND outbound allow rules.

questions.question: Main difference between EC2 Security Group and Network ACL?

options.text: SGs are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), instance-level. NACLs are stateless (explicit return rules needed), subnet-level.
SGs control inbound only. NACLs control both directions.
SGs are free, NACLs cost extra with deep packet inspection features.
SGs only support IP rules. NACLs support domain-based rules for blocking websites.

questions.explanation: Stateful vs stateless, instance vs subnet. The fundamental AWS networking distinction.

questions.question: App in private subnet needs internet (apt update). No public IP. What's needed?

options.text: Move to public subnet. Private subnets can never access internet.
NAT Gateway in public subnet. Private subnet route table points 0.0.0.0/0 to NAT. Outbound-only access.
Internet Gateway on VPC is sufficient. All subnets get internet with IGW.
VPN to office, route through office internet. Private subnets only reach internet via VPN.

questions.explanation: NAT Gateway enables outbound internet from private subnets without making instances publicly reachable.

questions.question: S3 bucket with versioning enabled. Accidentally delete important file. Recoverable?

options.text: No. S3 permanently removes on delete. Versioning only protects overwrites.
Yes. Versioning places 'delete marker' instead of removing data. Previous versions exist. Remove marker to restore.
Only within 24 hours. S3 keeps deleted files in temp buffer for one day.
Only with Object Lock. Standard versioning doesn't protect against deletions.

questions.explanation: Versioning: delete adds marker, objects remain as previous versions. Remove marker to restore.

questions.question: RDS PostgreSQL slow reads at peak. CPU at 40%. Most effective architectural fix?

options.text: Upgrade instance. At 40% CPU, approaching limit since RDS reserves 60% for internals.
Add read replica, route reads to it. 40% CPU = instance adequate, reads just need distribution.
Enable Multi-AZ. Standby handles read traffic during peak, doubling capacity.
Switch to Aurora Serverless for auto-scaling CPU and memory.

questions.explanation: Read replicas handle read scaling. Multi-AZ standby does NOT serve reads (failover only).

questions.question: Lambda with 3+ second cold starts. Code is 5MB. Best architectural solution?

options.text: Provisioned concurrency keeps instances pre-initialized. AWS warms them, eliminating cold starts.
CloudWatch timer invokes every 5 min with dummy event. Keeps container warm.
Move to ECS Fargate. Containers run continuously, no cold starts.
Increase memory to 10GB. More CPU allocated proportionally speeds initialization.

questions.explanation: Provisioned concurrency is AWS's official cold-start solution. B is unreliable hack. C changes architecture unnecessarily.

questions.question: `docker ps` shows nothing. `docker ps -a` shows 5 'Exited' containers. What does this mean?

options.text: Containers crashed. Docker stopped them and marked 'Exited' for investigation.
docker ps shows running only. docker ps -a shows all including stopped. 'Exited' = main process ended, not necessarily error.
Docker in maintenance mode. Paused all for auto-update, will restart them.
Containers frozen, still using resources. Use docker unpause to resume.

questions.explanation: docker ps = running. docker ps -a = all. Exited = process ended. Check exit code for error vs normal.

questions.question: 1.2GB Docker image for simple Python app. Single most impactful size reduction?

options.text: docker image prune after building removes unused layers, reducing 50-70%.
Switch base from python:3.11 to python:3.11-slim or alpine. Base image bloat is the #1 factor.
Add COMPRESS=true to Dockerfile. Built-in compression not enabled by default.
Combine all RUN commands. Each RUN creates a layer, and layer overhead causes large images.

questions.explanation: python:3.11 ~900MB, python:3.11-slim ~150MB, alpine ~50MB. Base image is the biggest factor.

questions.question: Change 1 line of code, rebuild. pip install reruns (3 min). How to avoid?

options.text: --cache-from flag pulls remote cache, skipping unchanged layers.
COPY requirements.txt and pip install BEFORE copying code. Cache is order-dependent — unchanged requirements = cached install.
Add pip install to .dockerignore to prevent cache invalidation.
docker buildx has smart dependency detection that skips pip install when only code changes.

questions.explanation: Docker layer caching is order-dependent. Copy deps first, install, then copy code. Most important Dockerfile pattern.

questions.question: Container works locally, 'exec format error' in production. Both Linux. Cause?

options.text: Built on ARM Mac (M1/M2/M3), production is AMD64. Architecture mismatch.
Production Docker version older, doesn't support Dockerfile syntax used.
Image corrupted during registry push. Re-push fixes it.
Production kernel too old. Container binaries need minimum kernel version.

questions.explanation: exec format error = architecture mismatch. Very common with ARM Macs + x86 servers. Fix: --platform linux/amd64

questions.question: Go binary works in Ubuntu builder stage, crashes 'not found' in Alpine final stage. File exists. Why?

options.text: Alpine uses musl libc, Ubuntu uses glibc. Binary dynamically linked against glibc. Fix: CGO_ENABLED=0 for static binary.
Alpine doesn't support Go binaries. Go only compatible with glibc distributions.
File permissions lost during COPY. Alpine has stricter defaults needing explicit chmod.
Alpine is case-sensitive, Ubuntu isn't. Binary name casing mismatch.

questions.explanation: Classic gotcha. CGO links against glibc dynamically. Alpine has musl. 'not found' = dynamic linker missing.

questions.question: Difference between a Kubernetes Deployment and a Pod?

options.text: Pod = single container. Deployment = group of Pods behind load balancer.
Pod = smallest unit (1+ containers). Deployment manages Pods: ensures replica count, handles rolling updates, recreates failed Pods.
Pod = single node, for dev. Deployment = multi-node, for production.
Pod = stateless. Deployment = stateful with persistent storage.

questions.explanation: Pod = smallest deployable unit. Deployment = controller managing pods via ReplicaSets.

questions.question: Pod in ImagePullBackOff status. What does this mean?

options.text: Scheduler can't place pod on node. BackOff = retrying placement with delays.
Can't download container image. Wrong name/tag, registry auth needed, or registry unreachable. Retries with backoff.
Container started but crashed. Image pulled but backing off before restart.
Node lacks disk space for image. BackOff waiting for space.

questions.explanation: ImagePullBackOff = image pull failed. Common: typo in name, missing imagePullSecrets, registry down. C describes CrashLoopBackOff.

questions.question: Pod has memory limit 512Mi. Tries to use 600Mi. What happens?

options.text: Kubernetes throttles memory to 512Mi, like CPU throttling. Pod continues.
Container OOMKilled. Unlike CPU (throttled), memory limits are hard-enforced by killing the process.
Nothing for 5 min. Then evicted if still over limit.
Pod borrows unused memory from other pods on same node.

questions.explanation: CPU = throttled (pod slows). Memory = hard kill (OOMKilled). Fundamental K8s distinction.

questions.question: Service type ClusterIP. Can external traffic reach it?

options.text: Yes — ClusterIP describes IP assignment, not accessibility.
No — ClusterIP is internal only. Use NodePort, LoadBalancer, or Ingress for external access.
Yes, if cluster firewall allows. ClusterIP listens on all node interfaces.
No — ClusterIP deprecated for external. K8s 1.25+ requires ExternalDNS.

questions.explanation: ClusterIP = internal only. External: NodePort, LoadBalancer, or Ingress.

questions.question: Deployment replicas:3 but kubectl shows 4 pods (3 Running + 1 Terminating). Problem?

options.text: Yes — two ReplicaSets competing, creating extras.
No — normal during rolling update or scale-down. New pods created before old terminated. Will resolve.
Yes — Terminating pod stuck. Force-delete needed.
No — K8s runs extra pod as hot standby for instant failover.

questions.explanation: Terminating pod during rolling update is normal. Graceful shutdown before removal.

questions.question: Updated ConfigMap used for pod env vars. Pods still show old values. Why?

options.text: ConfigMaps are immutable. Must delete and recreate, then restart pods.
Env vars from ConfigMaps set at startup, never auto-update. Must restart pods. Volume-mounted ConfigMaps DO update.
K8s caches ConfigMaps 5 min at node level. Wait for TTL.
Pod ServiceAccount lacks RBAC to read updated ConfigMap.

questions.explanation: Env vars are static after pod creation. Volume mounts update eventually. Restart needed for env vars.

questions.question: Pod has liveness AND readiness probes. Readiness probe fails. What happens?

options.text: Pod killed and restarted. Both probe failures trigger restart.
Pod keeps running but removed from Service endpoints (no traffic). Not restarted. Traffic resumes when readiness passes.
Pod enters CrashLoopBackOff with exponential backoff.
Nothing. Readiness probes are informational only.

questions.explanation: Readiness = traffic routing. Liveness = lifecycle. Different purposes. Most commonly misunderstood K8s concept.

questions.question: terraform plan shows '1 to add'. You apply. Run plan again. What shows?

options.text: '1 to add' again. Terraform recreates on every apply. Need terraform import.
'No changes. Infrastructure is up-to-date.' State matches code after apply.
'1 to change' — cloud provider added defaults that differ from code.
'1 to destroy' — resources scheduled for cleanup without lifecycle block.

questions.explanation: After apply, resource in state matches code. Plan finds no differences. Core Terraform loop.

questions.question: Two people run terraform apply simultaneously on same state. What happens?

options.text: Terraform auto-merges changes like Git conflict resolution.
Second silently overwrites first. No concurrent protection.
With remote state locking (S3+DynamoDB), second fails with lock error. Without locking, state corruption risk.
Both succeed, creating duplicates tracked in state.

questions.explanation: State locking prevents concurrent applies. Without it, corruption risk. Why remote state + locking is best practice.

questions.question: Existing manual AWS infra. Write Terraform code, run apply. What happens?

options.text: Terraform detects existing resources, auto-maps to code.
Tries to create NEW duplicates. Doesn't know existing resources. Use terraform import first.
Error: resources exist. Must delete before Terraform manages them.
Overwrites existing with code config. Automatic replacement.

questions.explanation: Terraform only knows resources in its state. Without import, treats code as new = creates duplicates.

questions.question: Module creates RDS + EC2. EC2 needs RDS endpoint in user data. How to pass it?

options.text: Hardcode endpoint. Both in same module, endpoint known at write time.
Reference: aws_db_instance.mydb.endpoint. Terraform resolves dependency — creates RDS first, passes endpoint to EC2.
Two separate applies: create RDS first, note endpoint, update EC2, apply again.
null_resource with local-exec to query AWS for endpoint after creation.

questions.explanation: Terraform dependency graph handles this. Reference creates implicit dependency: RDS first, then EC2.

questions.question: Accidentally terraform destroy on production. Code and state intact. Can you recover?

options.text: terraform apply recreates infra from code. But DATA (DB records, files) permanently lost without separate backups.
terraform state undo restores previous state. Terraform keeps state history for rollback.
terraform recover restores resources. AWS preserves deleted resources 7 days.
terraform import with old IDs from state history. Resources still exist but detached.

questions.explanation: Terraform recreates infrastructure (new IDs/IPs). DATA is gone forever without independent backups. IaC ≠ data backup.

questions.question: Primary purpose of a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Replace developers. Automates code writing so devs only review AI output.
Automate building, testing, deploying. Every change verified (CI) and optionally deployed (CD). Reduces errors, speeds delivery.
Backup code repository. Creates snapshots every commit for disaster recovery.
Monitor production. CI/CD = Continuous Inspection / Continuous Detection for error tracking.

questions.explanation: CI = build + test automatically. CD = deploy automatically or with approval.

questions.question: Tests pass locally, fail in CI with 'Connection refused' to test DB. Most likely cause?

options.text: CI sandboxed, can't access databases. Use SQLite for CI.
CI has no test DB running. Add database service container to CI config.
CI blocks all outbound connections for security.
Test DB credentials expired. CI uses separate creds needing rotation.

questions.explanation: CI runs in fresh environment. Must explicitly provide services (DB, Redis, etc.) via service containers.

questions.question: CD auto-deploys on main merge. Friday 5PM merge introduces bug. Prevention?

options.text: Disable CI/CD on Fridays. Block merges after Thursday.
Manual approval gate before production. Auto-deploy to staging, human approves production.
Run tests twice (before and after merge). Both pass = guaranteed bug-free.
24-hour delay between merge and production deploy.

questions.explanation: Manual approval for production is industry standard. Fast feedback to staging, human gate for prod.

questions.question: CI takes 8 min. npm install reruns when deps unchanged. Most effective fix?

options.text: Pipeline caching (actions/cache) keyed on package-lock.json hash. Unchanged deps = skip install.
Nightly pipeline for npm install. Daytime builds use yesterday's deps.
Pre-install all packages in custom CI runner image.
Run npm install parallel with build. Node supports concurrent install + compile.

questions.explanation: Cache keyed on lockfile hash. If unchanged, restore node_modules from cache. Standard CI optimization.

questions.question: Monorepo, 3 services. Every push builds all 3 even when 1 changed. How to optimize?

options.text: Split into 3 repos. Monorepos incompatible with efficient CI/CD.
Path-based triggers. Only build service when its directory files change (GitHub Actions paths filter).
Run all 3 parallel. Total time = one build, no need to skip.
Cache build output by code hash. Matching hash = reuse artifact.

questions.explanation: Path-based triggers = standard monorepo CI optimization. Build only what changed.

questions.question: Difference between monitoring and observability?

options.text: Same thing. Observability is modern marketing term for monitoring.
Monitoring = WHEN something is wrong (predefined alerts). Observability = WHY (metrics + logs + traces for unexpected issues).
Monitoring for infrastructure, observability for applications. Separate tools.
Monitoring is real-time, observability is historical analysis over weeks/months.

questions.explanation: Monitoring = known-unknowns. Observability = unknown-unknowns. Three pillars: metrics, logs, traces.

questions.question: Alert at 3AM: 'Memory 92% on node-3'. Been 90%+ for weeks. What's wrong with this alert?

options.text: Threshold too low. 92% normal. Set at 98%.
Lacks time-based conditions. Should alert on rate of change, not static threshold. Stable 90% is different from climbing to 90%.
Should only fire during business hours.
Prometheus miscounts. Linux page cache shows as used but is available.

questions.explanation: Static thresholds without trend context = noise. Alert on rate of change or approaching OOM, not absolute values.

questions.question: http_requests_total counter with status label. Correct PromQL for error rate %?

options.text: count(http_requests_total{status=~'5..'})
rate(http_requests_total{status=~'5..'}[5m]) / rate(http_requests_total[5m]) * 100
http_requests_total{status=~'5..'} / http_requests_total * 100
avg(http_requests_total{status=~'5..'})

questions.explanation: Must use rate() on counters (always increasing). Raw counter division (C) gives meaningless results.

questions.question: Dashboard: p99 latency 2.5s, p50 is 50ms. What does this pattern tell you?

options.text: Metrics broken. 50x ratio indicates measurement or histogram bucket error.
Most requests fast (50ms), ~1% very slow (2.5s+). Specific condition causes slowness: cache miss, heavy query, GC pauses.
Service overloaded. p99/p50 ratio grows linearly with load. Halving traffic fixes it.
p99 includes network latency, p50 only server time. Difference = network round-trip.

questions.explanation: Large p50/p99 gap = bimodal distribution. Most fast, tail is slow. Engineering task: find what causes the tail.

questions.question: Dev commits AWS key to public GitHub, immediately deletes commit + force pushes. Secret safe?

options.text: Yes — force push rewrites history. Safe if nobody forked.
No — GitHub caches commits, bots scan in seconds, fetched copies exist. Rotate key immediately.
Yes — GitHub auto-detects and redacts AWS keys before visibility.
Depends on speed. Under 60s, CDN hasn't propagated.

questions.explanation: Bots scan GitHub in real-time. Force-push doesn't delete caches/forks. Always rotate compromised credentials.

questions.question: App stores user passwords. Correct approach?

options.text: Encrypt with AES-256, decrypt on login to compare. Military-grade encryption.
Hash with bcrypt/Argon2, unique salt per user. Hash input on login, compare hashes. Never store actual password.
Store in separate encrypted table. Compromised main DB won't expose passwords.
Hash with SHA-256. Fast and widely supported. Speed important for login UX.

questions.explanation: Hashing (irreversible), slow algorithm (anti-brute-force), unique salt (anti-rainbow-table). AES is reversible. SHA-256 too fast.

questions.question: Principle of least privilege applied to K8s ServiceAccount?

options.text: Start with cluster-admin, gradually reduce. Avoids permission errors during dev.
Each ServiceAccount gets only exact permissions needed. Pod reading ConfigMaps only gets get/list on ConfigMaps.
Minimize number of ServiceAccounts. All namespace pods share one.
Assign at cluster level for visibility. Easier to audit than namespace-scoped.

questions.explanation: Minimum necessary permissions. If pod compromised, minimal SA limits blast radius.

questions.question: CI pulls node:18 without digest. Security audit flags it. Why?

options.text: Undigested images can't be vulnerability-scanned. Scanners need digest for CVE matching.
node:18 tag is mutable — can point to different content over time. Hijacked tag = compromised builds. Digest pinning ensures exact verified image.
Docker Hub rate-limits undigested pulls. Digests bypass limits via direct CDN.
Undigested images can't use content-addressable caching, wasting bandwidth.

questions.explanation: Tags are mutable pointers. Supply chain attacks exploit this. Digest = immutability = same verified image always.

questions.question: What does SLO stand for and why does it matter?

options.text: System Load Optimizer — auto-tunes server performance based on traffic patterns.
Service Level Objective — internal reliability target (e.g. 99.9% success). Creates error budget for acceptable failures.
Scheduled Latency Observation — monitoring schedule checking latency at intervals.
Software Lifecycle Overview — document describing product lifecycle.

questions.explanation: SLO = reliability target. Error budget = 100% - SLO. Balances reliability vs development speed.

questions.question: During incident, engineer starts reading code. Senior SRE says 'stop — mitigate first.' Why?

options.text: Reading code during incidents is a security risk.
Mitigation (rollback, scale up) restores service immediately. Root cause analysis after. 30 min debugging while users down > 2 min rollback.
Only original developer should read code. SRE monitors dashboards.
Code fixes need PR review, too slow during incident.

questions.explanation: Mitigate first, investigate second. Restore service (reduce MTTR), then postmortem for root cause.

questions.question: 99.9% SLO, currently at 99.85%. Product wants major feature deploy. What should happen?

options.text: Deploy anyway. SLO is guideline. Features = revenue.
Error budget exhausted (99.85% < 99.9%). Freeze features, focus reliability until budget recovers.
Split feature into smaller deploys. Smaller = always safer.
Deploy with 50% traffic. Halves SLO impact.

questions.explanation: Core SRE: burned error budget = reliability > features. Self-correcting system. Without discipline, SLOs are meaningless.

questions.question: EC2 bill $3K/month. Instances run 24/7, used only business hours. Simplest optimization?

options.text: Migrate to Lambda. Serverless charges only for compute time.
Schedule stop outside business hours. 168 hrs/week → ~50 hrs/week = ~70% savings.
Spot instances. 90% cheaper, fine for interruptible workloads.
3-year reserved instances. 60% savings from commitment.

questions.explanation: Instance scheduling = simplest, lowest-risk win. Stop when unused. ~70% savings immediately.

questions.question: $800/month data transfer. EC2 to S3, same region (should be free). Where's the cost?

options.text: Same-region S3 transfers aren't free. Common misconception.
Cross-AZ transfer. EC2 and S3 in different AZs charge per GB.
Traffic routes through NAT Gateway ($0.045/GB processing). Use S3 VPC Gateway Endpoint (free) instead.
S3 API request costs accumulated at high request volume.

questions.explanation: NAT Gateway processing fees on S3 traffic = common hidden cost. S3 VPC Gateway Endpoint is free, eliminates NAT.

questions.question: RDS db.r5.2xlarge, $1,200/month. Avg CPU 15%, memory 30%. Should you downsize?

options.text: Yes, immediately to db.r5.large. 15% CPU = massively over-provisioned.
Check PEAK utilization first. Averages misleading — peak might hit 70%+. Also check IOPS, connections, query latency.
No — RDS needs 70%+ headroom for Multi-AZ failover reconnection storms.
Switch to Aurora Serverless. Auto-scales, always cheaper.

questions.explanation: Averages hide peaks. 15% average might = 80% peak. Check peak CPU, IOPS, connections before downsizing.

questions.question: Server has two interfaces: eth0 (10.0.0.5) and eth1 (192.168.1.5). Traffic to 192.168.1.0/24 goes out eth0 and fails. Where to check?

options.text: Check /etc/hosts — it maps hostnames to interfaces and may be sending traffic out eth0 for that subnet.
Check the routing table (`ip route`). A missing or incorrect route for 192.168.1.0/24 sends traffic via the default route on eth0.
Check iptables rules. Firewall is probably NAT-ing 192.168.1.0/24 traffic to eth0's IP address.
Check /etc/network/interfaces. Linux binds subnets to interfaces at boot and won't change without restart.

questions.explanation: Routing table determines which interface handles traffic for each subnet. Missing route = default gateway used.

questions.question: Server with 8GB RAM and 2GB swap. RAM hits 100%, system is extremely slow but not crashing. Why isn't it crashing?

options.text: Linux compresses RAM contents automatically. It's slow because decompression takes time, but no crash needed.
Kernel is swapping pages to disk. System stays alive but disk I/O for memory operations makes it crawl.
Linux overcommits memory by default. Applications think they have more RAM than exists, so nothing crashes.
OOM killer is disabled by default. System queues memory requests until RAM frees up, causing slowness.

questions.explanation: Swap extends memory to disk. System survives but thrashes — constant swapping in/out causes extreme slowness.

questions.question: Service `myapp.service` has `After=postgresql.service` but no `Requires=`. PostgreSQL fails to start. What happens to myapp?

options.text: myapp also fails. After= implies dependency — if the target doesn't start, dependents are skipped.
myapp starts anyway, likely crashes. After= only sets ordering, not dependency. Without Requires=, systemd starts myapp regardless of PostgreSQL's state.
myapp waits indefinitely for PostgreSQL. After= creates a blocking dependency with no timeout.
Systemd starts myapp before PostgreSQL since the ordering constraint is void when the target fails.

questions.explanation: After= is ordering only ('if both start, start me second'). Requires= creates actual dependency. Common misconfiguration.

questions.question: You run `cat /proc/cpuinfo` on a container and see 64 cores, but the container is limited to 2 CPUs. Why?

options.text: /proc/cpuinfo shows the host's hardware. Containers share the host kernel, so /proc reflects host, not cgroup limits.
Container CPU limits only apply at runtime. /proc/cpuinfo is read at boot and cached with host values.
Docker mounts a virtual /proc that mirrors the host for compatibility. Use docker stats for real limits.
CPU limits are soft limits in containers. The container can burst to 64 cores when the host is idle.

questions.explanation: Containers share the host kernel. /proc is kernel-provided and shows host hardware. Cgroup limits are enforced separately.

questions.question: Process hangs at 100% CPU. `strace -p PID` shows thousands of repeated `open()` calls returning ENOENT. What's happening?

options.text: Filesystem corruption. open() failing with ENOENT on valid paths means inode table is damaged.
Process is in a tight retry loop trying to open a file that doesn't exist. No backoff means it burns CPU retrying instantly.
Kernel file descriptor limit reached. ENOENT is returned when the fd table is full.
SELinux blocking file access. ENOENT is the default denial response to hide file existence from the process.

questions.explanation: ENOENT = file not found. Tight loop retrying a missing file with no sleep/backoff = 100% CPU. Fix: add the file or fix the path.

questions.question: Server stuck at GRUB rescue prompt after disk replacement. OS disk is present. Most likely cause?

options.text: Disk order changed. GRUB config references old device names (sda/sdb). New disk layout shifted the OS disk identifier.
GRUB binary corrupted during disk hot-swap. Must reinstall GRUB from live USB.
BIOS reset boot order to default. Need to re-select the correct boot disk in BIOS.
Kernel modules for the new disk controller missing. GRUB can't access the disk without drivers.

questions.explanation: GRUB rescue = can't find config/kernel. Disk replacement changes device names. Fix: set root/prefix in GRUB rescue, then update grub.

questions.question: Suspicious process found on server. You check `/proc/PID/exe` and it points to `/tmp/.hidden`. File is deleted but process still runs. How?

options.text: Attacker used rootkit to hide the binary. /proc/PID/exe shows original path but file is cloaked from ls.
Linux keeps the binary mapped in memory after exec. Deleting the file removes the directory entry but the running process retains the inode.
Process was started with memfd_create, never had a real file. /proc/PID/exe shows a fake path.
Systemd caches service binaries in RAM. Even deleted files run until systemd daemon-reload is called.

questions.explanation: Running processes hold inode references. Deleting the file only removes the name — binary stays mapped in memory until process exits.

questions.question: Client connects to server, connection works, but first request takes 3 seconds. Subsequent requests are instant. Why?

options.text: Server cold-starts on first request. Application loads into memory, making first connection slow.
TCP 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) adds round-trip latency on first connect. After that, the connection is reused (keep-alive).
DNS resolution on first request is slow. Result cached for subsequent requests.
Firewall inspects first packet deeply. Once connection is classified as safe, subsequent packets fast-tracked.

questions.explanation: 3 seconds points to DNS timeout/retry, not TCP handshake (milliseconds). DNS caches the result for TTL duration.

questions.question: You update a DNS A record, but users still reach the old IP 4 hours later. DNS provider shows the new IP. What's happening?

options.text: DNS propagation takes 24-48 hours across all global DNS servers regardless of settings.
The old record's TTL hasn't expired in caching resolvers. Resolvers serve cached responses until TTL counts down to zero.
Users' browsers cache DNS independently of system DNS. Clearing browser cache fixes it immediately.
ISP DNS servers only refresh records once daily at midnight. Users must switch to 8.8.8.8 for faster updates.

questions.explanation: TTL tells resolvers how long to cache. Old TTL was likely hours. Resolvers won't re-query until TTL expires.

questions.question: Website moved from /old-page to /new-page. SEO team says use 301, not 302. Why does it matter?

options.text: 301 = permanent move. Search engines transfer ranking to new URL. 302 = temporary, search engines keep indexing old URL.
301 is faster because browsers skip the redirect after first visit. 302 always goes through the server.
301 preserves POST data on redirect. 302 converts POST to GET, losing form submissions.
301 works cross-domain, 302 only works same-domain. Moving pages across domains requires 301.

questions.explanation: 301 = permanent: search engines move link equity to new URL. 302 = temporary: old URL keeps its ranking.

questions.question: VPN users report some websites load but others show incomplete pages or time out. MTU is 1500 on the VPN interface. What's the issue?

options.text: VPN encryption adds overhead, making effective MTU smaller. Packets exceeding tunnel MTU are dropped if DF bit is set. Lower MTU to ~1400.
VPN splits TCP connections, reassembling at the server side. Some sites block split-TCP proxies for security.
VPN DNS is resolving to different server IPs than direct connections. Some sites serve different content via VPN.
Websites with HTTPS fail because VPN certificate interferes with TLS handshake on large certificate chains.

questions.explanation: VPN encapsulation adds headers (20-60 bytes). 1500-byte packets become oversized inside the tunnel. DF flag prevents fragmentation = drops.

questions.question: traceroute shows hops 1-5 with low latency, hop 6 shows `* * *`, then hops 7-10 respond normally. Is hop 6 the problem?

options.text: Yes — hop 6 is a failing router. Packets are being re-routed around it, adding latency.
Not necessarily. Many routers drop ICMP/UDP traceroute probes (rate-limited or firewalled) but forward actual traffic fine.
Yes — `* * *` means 100% packet loss at that hop. Traffic after it is from a different path.
Not necessarily — hop 6 is a load balancer. They don't respond to traceroute by design but pass traffic.

questions.explanation: Stars mean the router didn't reply to probes, not that traffic is blocked. Many routers deprioritize or drop ICMP. Check final destination.

questions.question: Firewall rule allows 10.0.0.0/24. Colleague asks if 10.0.0.200 is covered. How many hosts does /24 include?

options.text: 24 hosts (10.0.0.1 through 10.0.0.24). The /24 means 24 usable addresses in the range.
256 addresses (10.0.0.0–10.0.0.255), 254 usable. /24 = 24 network bits, 8 host bits. 10.0.0.200 is covered.
Exactly 24 subnets of /32 each. 10.0.0.200 falls outside those 24 subnets.
Unlimited — /24 is a wildcard matching any IP starting with 10.0.0. Includes 10.0.0.200 and beyond.

questions.explanation: /24 = 24 bits for network, 8 bits for hosts = 2^8 = 256 addresses (254 usable). 10.0.0.200 falls within the range.

questions.question: Two offices behind NAT need site-to-site VPN. IPSec fails to establish. WireGuard works. Why?

options.text: IPSec ESP protocol (IP protocol 50) isn't TCP or UDP — many NAT devices can't track it. WireGuard uses UDP, which NAT handles natively.
IPSec requires static public IPs on both sides. NAT assigns dynamic ports, breaking the pre-shared key exchange.
WireGuard encrypts the handshake, so NAT can't inspect and block it. IPSec handshake is cleartext and gets filtered.
IPSec needs port forwarding on both NAT routers. WireGuard auto-discovers ports via STUN protocol.

questions.explanation: IPSec ESP is protocol 50 (not TCP/UDP). NAT traversal requires NAT-T (ESP in UDP). WireGuard is UDP-native, traversing NAT easily.

questions.question: Users get 'certificate not trusted' on your site. Certificate is valid, not expired, correct domain. Intermediate CA cert missing from server. Why does this break trust?

options.text: Browsers can't verify the chain: your cert → intermediate CA → root CA. Missing intermediate means no path to a trusted root. Some browsers cache intermediates, masking the issue.
Missing intermediate triggers certificate revocation check, which times out and defaults to 'untrusted' for safety.
Intermediate cert contains the encryption keys. Without it, TLS handshake fails with decryption error shown as 'not trusted'.
Browsers require all certificates in the chain to have matching key lengths. Missing intermediate breaks key length validation.

questions.explanation: TLS trust requires a complete chain to a root CA. Missing intermediate = broken chain. Chrome may work (cached), Firefox fails (strict).

questions.question: You're mid-feature on branch `feature-x`. Urgent bug report comes in. You need to switch to `main` but have uncommitted changes. Quickest safe approach?

options.text: Commit with message 'WIP' then switch. You can always amend later.
`git stash` saves uncommitted changes temporarily. Switch to main, fix bug, switch back, `git stash pop` to restore work.
Copy changed files to a backup folder, `git checkout -- .` to discard, switch branches, paste files back later.
`git checkout main` works directly. Git carries uncommitted changes across branches automatically.

questions.explanation: git stash is designed for this: temporarily shelve changes without committing. Pop restores them later.

questions.question: You add `*.log` to .gitignore, but `debug.log` (already tracked) still shows in `git status`. Why?

options.text: .gitignore only prevents untracked files from being added. Already-tracked files are unaffected. Run `git rm --cached debug.log` to untrack it.
.gitignore has a syntax error. Wildcard `*` must be escaped as `\*` for glob patterns to work.
.gitignore changes require a commit before they take effect. Commit the .gitignore file first.
.gitignore only works in the root directory. Subdirectory log files need a separate .gitignore in each folder.

questions.explanation: .gitignore only affects untracked files. Already-tracked files must be explicitly untracked with git rm --cached.

questions.question: After `git checkout abc123` (a commit hash), Git warns 'detached HEAD'. You make 2 commits. Then `git checkout main`. What happens to those commits?

options.text: Commits are merged into main automatically when you switch back.
Commits become orphaned — no branch points to them. They'll be garbage collected eventually unless you create a branch from them first.
Commits are lost immediately. Detached HEAD mode discards commits on checkout.
Commits stay at that hash. You can always return to them by checking out abc123 again.

questions.explanation: Detached HEAD commits aren't on any branch. Without a branch/tag reference, git gc will eventually remove them. Git warns you for this reason.

questions.question: Production hotfix on commit `abc123` in `main`. You need just that fix in your feature branch (30 commits behind main). `git merge main` would bring all 30. Better approach?

options.text: `git cherry-pick abc123` applies only that commit to your branch. Targeted — no other main commits come along.
`git rebase main` and then revert the 29 unwanted commits. Keeps history linear.
`git merge main --squash` merges all 30 as one commit, then you delete the unneeded changes manually.
Create a new branch from abc123 and merge that branch. Limits scope to just that commit's history.

questions.explanation: Cherry-pick applies a specific commit to current branch. Perfect for pulling individual fixes without merging everything.

questions.question: PR has 15 commits: 'WIP', 'fix typo', 'actually fix it', 'debug', 'done'. Reviewer asks to squash. Why?

options.text: Squashing reduces repo size. Each commit stores a full copy of changed files, so fewer commits = less storage.
Noisy commits obscure intent in git log/blame. Squashing into one meaningful commit makes history reviewable and bisectable.
GitHub can't handle large commit counts per PR. Squashing prevents API rate-limit errors on merge.
Squashing is required for CI to run on the merged result. Multiple commits trigger separate CI runs each.

questions.explanation: Clean history matters for git log, blame, and bisect. 'WIP' and 'fix typo' commits add noise, not value.

questions.question: Colleague rebased a shared branch and force-pushed. Your next `git pull` shows dozens of conflicts on files you never touched. Why?

options.text: Rebase rewrote commit SHAs. Your local branch has original commits, remote has rewritten ones. Git sees them as different changes to the same files.
Force-push corrupted the branch history. The only fix is to delete local branch and re-clone.
Rebase re-ordered commits, changing file timestamps. Git uses timestamps for conflict detection.
Force-push bypassed merge checks. Conflicts would have been caught by GitHub's merge button.

questions.explanation: Rebase creates new commits (new SHAs). Your old commits + new remote commits = duplicate changes = conflicts. Never rebase shared branches.

questions.question: Something broke between v2.0 (known good, 200 commits ago) and HEAD. Manually checking each commit is impractical. Best git tool?

options.text: `git log --oneline v2.0..HEAD` and read commit messages to guess which one broke it.
`git bisect` — binary search across commits. Tests middle commit, halves range each step. Finds the culprit in ~8 steps out of 200.
`git diff v2.0..HEAD` to see all changes, then manually identify the breaking change.
`git blame` on the broken file to find who changed it last.

questions.explanation: git bisect does binary search: log2(200) ≈ 8 steps. Mark commits good/bad, git narrows it down automatically.

questions.question: Team wants to enforce: no commits to main without passing tests. GitHub branch protection isn't available (self-hosted). Solution?

options.text: Server-side `pre-receive` hook. Runs on push, rejects if tests fail. Can't be bypassed by developers since it runs on the server.
Client-side `pre-commit` hook in the repo. Developers install it locally, runs tests before each commit.
Set main branch to read-only in filesystem permissions. Developers push to feature branches only.
CI webhook that auto-reverts bad commits to main within 60 seconds of push.

questions.explanation: Server-side pre-receive hooks enforce policy at push time. Client-side hooks can be skipped (--no-verify). Server-side can't be bypassed.

questions.question: Monorepo uses git submodules for shared libraries. Developer clones the repo, runs the app, gets 'module not found' errors. Submodule directories are empty. Why?

options.text: `git clone` doesn't fetch submodules by default. Need `git clone --recurse-submodules` or `git submodule update --init` after clone.
Submodules require separate SSH keys. Without configured access to each submodule's repo, directories stay empty.
Submodule commits are ahead of what the parent repo references. Version mismatch leaves directories empty.
.gitmodules file missing from the clone. Without it, git doesn't know where to fetch submodule contents.

questions.explanation: Submodules are not fetched by default on clone. The directory exists (tracked as a pointer) but content requires explicit init + update.

questions.question: Bash script: `filename="my report.pdf"; rm $filename`. It deletes files named `my` and `report.pdf` instead of the actual file. Fix?

options.text: Use single quotes: `rm '$filename'`. Single quotes prevent word splitting on spaces.
Double-quote the variable: `rm "$filename"`. Without quotes, bash splits on spaces, treating each word as a separate argument.
Escape the space in the assignment: `filename="my\ report.pdf"`. Backslash preserves the space.
Use braces: `rm ${filename}`. Braces group the variable, preventing word splitting.

questions.explanation: Unquoted variables undergo word splitting. Double quotes preserve spaces as part of the value. Most common bash bug.

questions.question: Script needs to pass a multi-line SQL query to `psql`. Using multiple echo commands is messy. Cleaner approach?

options.text: Store SQL in a variable with \n characters and echo -e to interpret them.
Use a heredoc: `psql <<EOF ... EOF`. Passes multi-line text as stdin, keeping SQL readable inline in the script.
Write SQL to a temp file first, then `psql -f tempfile`. Only reliable way to handle multi-line input.
Use backticks to wrap the entire SQL block. Backticks preserve whitespace and newlines in bash.

questions.explanation: Heredoc (<<EOF...EOF) passes multi-line text to stdin cleanly. Standard bash feature for inline multi-line input.

questions.question: Cron job `0 3 * * 1` runs a backup script. Team asks: when does it execute?

options.text: Every 3 hours on the 1st of each month. Format: minute hour day month weekday.
3:00 AM every Monday. Fields: minute(0) hour(3) day(*) month(*) weekday(1=Monday).
1:03 AM every day. First field is hour, second is minute in cron format.
3:00 AM on January 1st only. The 1 means January, asterisks mean 'first occurrence'.

questions.explanation: Cron: minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week. 0 3 * * 1 = minute 0, hour 3, any day/month, weekday 1 (Monday).

questions.question: Deployment script runs `./migrate.sh && ./start-app.sh`. Migration fails with exit code 1. What happens to start-app.sh?

options.text: start-app.sh runs anyway. && is cosmetic in bash, just separates commands on one line.
start-app.sh is skipped. && means 'run next only if previous succeeded (exit 0)'. Exit 1 = failure = stop.
start-app.sh runs but inherits the exit code 1, causing it to behave differently.
Bash prompts the user whether to continue after the failure.

questions.explanation: && is logical AND: only proceeds if left side returns 0. Non-zero exit code stops the chain. Use || for 'run on failure'.

questions.question: New developer installs project dependencies globally with `pip install`. Works on their machine, breaks in production. Senior says 'use a virtualenv'. Why?

options.text: virtualenv encrypts packages for production security. Global installs are unencrypted.
Global installs mix dependencies across projects. Project A needing requests==2.28 and Project B needing requests==2.31 conflict. Virtualenv isolates per-project.
pip install only works for development. Production requires virtualenv for packages to be importable.
virtualenv compiles packages for the target OS. Global pip installs are platform-specific and non-portable.

questions.explanation: Virtualenvs isolate dependencies per project. Without them, version conflicts between projects are inevitable.

questions.question: Bash script works in testing, fails silently in production. No error output. You suspect a variable is empty. Fastest way to catch these issues before deployment?

options.text: Add `echo` before every variable use to print its value. Remove the echoes after debugging.
Run `shellcheck script.sh`. It statically analyzes bash scripts, catching unquoted variables, unused vars, and common pitfalls without executing.
Run with `bash -x script.sh` in production to see each command before execution.
Add `set -e` at the top. It catches all empty variable errors and exits with a clear message.

questions.explanation: ShellCheck is a static analyzer for shell scripts. Catches bugs like unquoted variables, SC2086, without running the script. CI-friendly.

questions.question: Script creates a temp file, processes data, but on Ctrl+C the temp file is left behind. How to ensure cleanup on any exit?

options.text: Use `mktemp` — it auto-deletes temp files when the script exits, handling all signals automatically.
`trap 'rm -f $tmpfile' EXIT` — trap registers a cleanup function that runs on any exit: normal, error, or signal (SIGINT/SIGTERM).
Write to /dev/shm instead of /tmp. RAM-backed filesystem auto-clears on reboot.
Use `nohup` to make the script immune to Ctrl+C. Script always completes, reaching the cleanup line at the end.

questions.explanation: trap EXIT runs cleanup on any exit condition. Essential pattern for scripts creating temp files, lock files, or other resources.

questions.question: CI pipeline gets JSON from an API: `{"status":"deployed","version":"2.1.3"}`. Need to extract version in a bash script. `grep` and `awk` hacks keep breaking. Robust solution?

options.text: `echo $json | python3 -c 'import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["version"])'` — Python handles all JSON edge cases.
`echo $json | jq -r '.version'` — jq is a dedicated JSON parser for the command line. Handles nested objects, arrays, escaping correctly.
`echo $json | sed 's/.*version":"\(.*\)".*/\1/'` — regex with sed is the standard Unix approach for text extraction.
`echo $json | cut -d'"' -f8` — split on quotes, version is always the 8th field in this JSON structure.

questions.explanation: jq is purpose-built for JSON in shell scripts. Regex/cut break on formatting changes, nested structures, or escaped characters.

questions.question: A developer's IAM user has an explicit Allow for s3:PutObject, but a Service Control Policy on the AWS account denies all S3 actions. What happens when they upload a file?

options.text: The upload succeeds because IAM policies take precedence over SCPs — IAM identity policies are evaluated first and the explicit allow grants access.
The upload is denied because SCPs set the maximum permissions boundary — even with an IAM allow, the SCP deny overrides it.
The upload succeeds because SCPs only apply to the root account user — individual IAM users are not affected by service control policies.
The result depends on the S3 bucket policy — the bucket policy is the final arbiter when IAM and SCP conflict.

questions.explanation: SCPs define the maximum available permissions for accounts in an organization. Even if an IAM policy explicitly allows an action, it is denied if the SCP does not also allow it.

questions.question: Your ASG just launched 3 instances due to high CPU. CPU is still high 60 seconds later, but the default cooldown is 300 seconds. What happens?

options.text: The ASG launches more instances immediately because the CloudWatch alarm is still in ALARM state — active alarms always trigger scaling regardless of cooldowns.
The ASG ignores the cooldown because CPU remains above threshold — cooldowns only apply when metrics return to normal levels.
The ASG waits until the cooldown expires before evaluating whether to scale again — this prevents thrashing from rapid successive scaling actions.
The ASG terminates the newly launched instances and tries a larger instance type — it assumes the current type cannot handle the load.

questions.explanation: The ASG cooldown period prevents additional scaling actions until it expires, giving newly launched instances time to start handling traffic and stabilize the metric.

questions.question: Your production RDS MySQL has Multi-AZ enabled. A reporting dashboard is adding heavy read traffic. Should you use the Multi-AZ standby or add a Read Replica?

options.text: Use the Multi-AZ standby for reads since it's already provisioned — the standby is a fully functional copy that can serve read queries simultaneously.
Route reads to the standby via the reader endpoint that RDS automatically creates for Multi-AZ deployments — this is how AWS intends read scaling to work.
Add a Read Replica because the Multi-AZ standby cannot serve read traffic — the standby only activates during failover and does not accept connections.
Switch from Multi-AZ to a Read Replica since RDS doesn't allow both — you must choose between high availability and read scaling.

questions.explanation: The Multi-AZ standby is a passive copy used only for automatic failover. It cannot serve read traffic. Read Replicas are the correct mechanism for offloading read queries.

questions.question: VPC A is peered with VPC B, and VPC B is peered with VPC C. An instance in VPC A needs to reach an instance in VPC C. Will the traffic flow?

options.text: Yes, traffic flows through VPC B since both peering connections exist — VPC peering is transitive when there is a common intermediary VPC.
No, VPC peering is non-transitive — you must create a direct peering connection between VPC A and VPC C for them to communicate.
Yes, if you add routes in VPC B to forward between peerings — VPC B can act as a transit VPC with proper route table entries.
Yes, after a few minutes of route propagation — AWS needs time to update routing tables across all three VPCs.

questions.explanation: VPC peering is non-transitive. Traffic cannot flow A→B→C even if both peerings exist. A direct peering (or Transit Gateway) between A and C is required.

questions.question: Your team runs batch processing jobs that tolerate interruption and restart. Jobs run 2-6 hours daily. Which EC2 purchasing option gives the best savings?

options.text: Reserved Instances with a 1-year term — the predictable daily schedule qualifies for reserved pricing, which gives the deepest discount over on-demand.
Spot Instances since the workload is interruption-tolerant — Spot can be up to 90% cheaper than on-demand, and batch jobs can gracefully handle interruptions.
On-Demand with Compute Savings Plans — Savings Plans provide automatic discounts without committing to specific instance types or availability zones.
Dedicated Hosts for consistent performance — dedicated hardware eliminates noisy-neighbor issues that could slow down batch processing.

questions.explanation: Spot Instances offer up to 90% savings and are ideal for interruption-tolerant batch workloads. Reserved Instances suit steady 24/7 loads, not intermittent daily jobs.

questions.question: An IAM role has an inline policy allowing s3:* on all resources, but its permissions boundary only allows s3:GetObject. A user assumes this role and calls s3:PutObject. What's the result?

options.text: The call succeeds because the inline policy explicitly grants s3:* — inline policies are the highest priority and cannot be overridden by boundaries.
The call is denied because permissions boundaries restrict the maximum permissions — effective permissions are the intersection of the identity policy and the boundary.
The call succeeds because permissions boundaries only apply to IAM users, not assumed roles — roles use trust policies instead of boundaries.
The call is denied only if there's also an explicit deny in a bucket policy — permissions boundaries alone cannot restrict actions without a supporting resource policy.

questions.explanation: Permissions boundaries define the maximum permissions an entity can have. The effective permissions are the intersection of identity-based policies and the boundary, so s3:PutObject is denied.

questions.question: You run a container with --network host on Linux. The app inside listens on port 8080. How do you access it from the host?

options.text: Use localhost:8080 because host networking shares the host's network namespace — the container's ports are directly bound to the host without any mapping.
You still need -p 8080:8080 even with host networking — Docker always requires explicit port mapping to expose container services.
Access it via the container's bridge IP on port 8080 — host networking still assigns a bridge IP but bypasses NAT translation.
Run docker port to find the randomly assigned host port — host networking uses ephemeral port allocation by default.

questions.explanation: With --network host, the container shares the host's network stack directly. No port mapping is needed; the app's port 8080 is accessible on the host's localhost:8080.

questions.question: A developer mounts local source code into a container with -v /home/dev/src:/usr/src/app for live reloading. What type of mount is this?

options.text: A Docker named volume — Docker manages the storage location, and the absolute path syntax automatically creates a named volume.
A bind mount because it maps a specific host path to a container path — bind mounts give the container direct access to the host filesystem.
A tmpfs mount since development files should only exist in memory — this provides faster I/O for live reload workflows.
An anonymous volume that Docker creates automatically — using an absolute path without a name causes Docker to generate a random volume.

questions.explanation: When an absolute host path is used with -v, Docker creates a bind mount that maps the host directory directly into the container, allowing live file changes to be reflected.

questions.question: Your container runs as root by default. A vulnerability allows remote code execution inside the container. What is the primary security concern?

options.text: The attacker gains root on the host immediately — container root is identical to host root with no isolation layer between them.
Root inside the container increases the blast radius of a container escape — root can modify system files, load kernel modules, and access privileged kernel features.
There is no additional risk because container namespaces fully isolate root — the root user inside a container has zero capabilities outside it.
The attacker can only read files but cannot execute commands — container root is restricted to read-only operations by default for security.

questions.explanation: Running as root inside a container significantly increases the impact of a container escape exploit, as root has elevated privileges that can be leveraged to break out of isolation.

questions.question: Your docker-compose.yml has a web service with depends_on: [db]. On deploy, the web app crashes because the database isn't ready to accept connections. Why?

options.text: depends_on only controls startup order, not readiness — it starts the db container first but doesn't wait for the database process inside to be ready.
depends_on was removed in Compose v3 — this directive is ignored entirely, so both containers start simultaneously.
The db container must expose a healthcheck port for depends_on to detect readiness — without an explicitly exposed port, Docker can't verify the service.
depends_on does wait for readiness but uses a 5-second default timeout — the database likely takes longer than 5 seconds to finish initializing.

questions.explanation: depends_on only ensures the container starts, not that the service inside is ready. Use depends_on with condition: service_healthy and a healthcheck to wait for actual readiness.

questions.question: You push two different images to the same registry, both built FROM ubuntu:22.04. The first image took 250MB in the registry. How much additional space does the second image likely use?

options.text: Another 250MB because each image is stored as a complete independent artifact — registries keep full copies of every image pushed.
Only the size of the unique layers because registries deduplicate shared layers — the ubuntu:22.04 base layers are stored once and referenced by both images.
Exactly 0MB because the registry detects it as a duplicate image — identical base images are aliased together with no additional storage cost.
Around 500MB because the registry adds versioning metadata — each push creates additional manifest and history data that roughly doubles the size.

questions.explanation: Container registries store layers by content-addressable digest. Shared base layers (like ubuntu:22.04) are stored once, so only the new unique layers consume additional space.

questions.question: Two containers on the same custom Docker bridge network can resolve each other's names, but TCP connections time out. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: Docker's embedded DNS is returning a stale cached IP from a previous container incarnation — bridge network DNS doesn't invalidate entries when containers restart.
The target container's application is bound to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0 — binding to localhost inside a container rejects connections from other containers.
Custom bridge networks don't support inter-container communication by default — you need to pass --icc=true to the Docker daemon to enable it.
Docker's iptables rules block traffic between containers on the same bridge — each container gets a dedicated iptables chain that defaults to DROP.

questions.explanation: If the application binds to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0, it only accepts connections from within the same network namespace. Other containers see DNS resolve but connections time out.

questions.question: A multi-stage Dockerfile copies a compiled Go binary into a FROM scratch final stage. A security scanner reports zero CVEs. Should you fully trust the result?

options.text: Yes, FROM scratch means there are no OS packages that could have vulnerabilities — the image contains only your binary, so the scanner has nothing to flag.
Not entirely, because the binary may contain vulnerabilities in statically linked libraries — most scanners only check OS packages and miss compiled-in dependencies.
Yes, multi-stage builds automatically strip vulnerable dependencies during the copy — COPY --from only transfers clean, validated artifacts.
Not entirely, because FROM scratch images cannot be scanned at all — the scanner returns zero CVEs simply because it fails to parse the image format.

questions.explanation: Scanners typically analyze OS packages and known library files. A statically compiled binary can embed vulnerable C libraries or Go modules that the scanner cannot detect.

questions.question: A pod using a PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted. The PV's reclaim policy is Retain. What happens to the stored data?

options.text: The data is preserved on the PersistentVolume and must be manually cleaned up — Retain keeps the volume and its data intact even after the PVC is deleted.
The data is deleted when the PVC is released — Retain only prevents deletion while the pod is actively running.
The data is automatically backed up to a VolumeSnapshot before deletion — Retain triggers a snapshot workflow before releasing the volume.
The data is migrated to the cluster's default StorageClass — Retain moves data to prevent loss rather than keeping it on the original volume.

questions.explanation: The Retain reclaim policy preserves the PV and its data after the PVC is deleted. An administrator must manually reclaim, clean up, or reuse the volume.

questions.question: A developer needs to list pods only in the staging namespace with no access to other namespaces. Which RBAC resources scope this correctly?

options.text: A ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding — ClusterRoles can be namespace-restricted when you set the namespace field on the binding.
A Role and RoleBinding in the staging namespace — Roles are namespace-scoped and only grant permissions within that specific namespace.
A ClusterRoleBinding with a namespace selector — namespace selectors on ClusterRoleBindings limit which namespaces the permissions apply to.
A Role in staging bound with a ClusterRoleBinding — this combination grants the namespace-level role across all namespaces.

questions.explanation: A Role is namespace-scoped, granting permissions only in the namespace it's created in. A RoleBinding in the same namespace binds it to the user, limiting access to staging only.

questions.question: Your HPA targets 50% CPU utilization. Pods are at 80% CPU but the HPA won't scale beyond 5 replicas. What's the most likely reason?

options.text: The HPA has reached its maxReplicas setting of 5 — the HPA respects the configured ceiling even if the target metric is still exceeded.
The cluster has no schedulable nodes with enough CPU — the HPA creates pods but the scheduler can't place them, causing a silent cap.
HPA has a built-in default limit of 5 replicas per deployment — this safety cap prevents runaway scaling and must be explicitly raised.
The metrics-server is returning cached data showing lower CPU usage — HPA won't scale if it detects stale metrics older than 60 seconds.

questions.explanation: The HPA's maxReplicas field sets a hard ceiling on the number of replicas. Even if CPU exceeds the target, the HPA will not create replicas beyond this limit.

questions.question: You apply a NetworkPolicy with podSelector: {} and an empty ingress array to a namespace. What happens to incoming traffic to pods in that namespace?

options.text: All ingress traffic is allowed because an empty rule list means no restrictions — specifying no ingress rules is equivalent to allowing everything.
All ingress traffic is denied because selecting all pods with no ingress rules creates a default-deny posture — pods with no allow rules reject all incoming connections.
Only traffic from pods in the same namespace is allowed — a NetworkPolicy without explicit rules defaults to permitting intra-namespace communication.
The policy has no effect because podSelector: {} is invalid syntax — you must specify at least one label to match pods.

questions.explanation: A NetworkPolicy with podSelector: {} selects all pods. An empty ingress list means no ingress is allowed, establishing a default-deny posture for incoming traffic.

questions.question: You delete an operator deployment, but the Database custom resources it managed still exist in the cluster. What happens to the actual databases?

options.text: The databases are immediately deleted because the CRD's finalizer triggers cleanup — Kubernetes garbage-collects dependent resources when their controller is removed.
The custom resources remain but the databases become unmanaged — without the operator's reconciliation loop, no controller enforces the desired state.
Kubernetes automatically restarts the operator to handle orphaned resources — CRD registration ensures the managing controller is always running.
The Database CRs enter a Pending state until the operator returns — Kubernetes marks controller-dependent resources as pending when their controller is absent.

questions.explanation: Deleting the operator stops the reconciliation loop. Existing CRs remain in the API server and the actual databases continue running, but drift from desired state is no longer corrected.

questions.question: Your shared Terraform VPC module is used by 10 projects. You need to update it without risking breakage in existing deployments. What should you do?

options.text: Update the module in place since terraform plan will show the changes — each project team can review the diff and decide whether to apply.
Version the module and release a new version — existing projects stay pinned to the old version and upgrade on their own schedule.
Fork the module into each project's repository — this ensures every project has an independent copy that won't be affected by upstream changes.
Use terraform workspace to isolate the module changes — workspaces prevent module updates from affecting other environments.

questions.explanation: Versioning modules using Git tags or a registry allows consumers to pin to a specific version. New versions can be adopted incrementally without forcing changes on existing users.

questions.question: A teammate's PR is failing CI because Terraform files have inconsistent indentation and spacing. What command should they run?

options.text: terraform validate to fix syntax and formatting — validate auto-corrects both syntax errors and style inconsistencies in HCL files.
terraform fmt to rewrite files to canonical style — fmt automatically normalizes indentation and spacing to Terraform's standard format.
terraform plan to detect formatting problems — plan includes a formatting check phase before generating the execution plan.
terraform init to reset file formatting — init re-downloads providers and normalizes all configuration files to default formatting.

questions.explanation: terraform fmt rewrites Terraform configuration files to a canonical format and style. It handles indentation, spacing, and alignment automatically.

questions.question: You need to install Nginx and deploy different config files across 50 servers grouped by role. Should you use Terraform or Ansible?

options.text: Terraform because its provisioners handle configuration management — remote-exec provisioners can run installation scripts at scale across server groups.
Ansible because it specializes in configuration management — Ansible excels at package installation, service management, and templating configs per host group.
Terraform because a single tool for infrastructure and configuration reduces complexity — managing everything in one tool avoids integration overhead.
Either tool works identically since both handle configuration management with equal capability — the choice is purely a matter of team preference.

questions.explanation: Ansible is purpose-built for configuration management with features like inventory groups, Jinja2 templates, and idempotent modules. Terraform provisioners are a last resort, not a CM solution.

questions.question: Your team uses Terraform workspaces to separate dev, staging, and prod. A junior engineer runs terraform destroy in the wrong workspace, deleting prod. How should you restructure?

options.text: Add workspace-level RBAC within Terraform CLI to prevent unauthorized destroys — Terraform supports role-based access control per workspace natively.
Use separate directories or repos per environment with distinct state files — physical separation prevents accidental cross-environment operations.
Keep workspaces but add a terraform.tfvars lock file per workspace — lock files can prevent destructive commands on protected workspaces.
Switch to Terraform Cloud which blocks destroy on production workspaces by default — Terraform Cloud has built-in workspace protection that prevents all destroys.

questions.explanation: Separate directories or repos per environment provide the strongest isolation. Workspaces share code and differ only in state, making accidental cross-environment operations too easy.

questions.question: You want your Terraform config to dynamically look up the latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI ID instead of hardcoding it. What should you use?

options.text: A variable with a default set to the current AMI ID — variables allow easy updates by changing the default value when the AMI rotates.
A data source to query AWS for the most recent matching AMI — data sources fetch live information from providers at plan time without creating resources.
A local value that computes the AMI ID from the region name — locals can derive the correct AMI using string interpolation and region-based mappings.
A Terraform resource that creates a custom AMI from a base image — this ensures you always have a controlled, known AMI ID in your account.

questions.explanation: The aws_ami data source queries the AWS API at plan time with filters like name and owner, returning the most recent matching AMI ID dynamically.

questions.question: You run terraform validate and it passes. Then terraform plan fails because a VPC ID in your config doesn't exist in AWS. Why didn't validate catch this?

options.text: validate has a known limitation with networking resources — it skips VPC-related validation because the AWS API is too complex to query offline.
validate only checks HCL syntax and internal consistency — it doesn't contact the cloud provider to verify that referenced resource IDs actually exist.
validate only checks files modified since the last apply — unchanged files containing errors are skipped for performance reasons.
validate uses a cached copy of the state file — it passed because the VPC existed when the state was last refreshed.

questions.explanation: terraform validate checks configuration syntax, attribute names, and type correctness without accessing remote state or APIs. Only plan and apply contact the provider to verify real resources.

questions.question: You add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to an RDS instance. A teammate runs terraform destroy on the entire stack. What happens?

options.text: The RDS instance is skipped and everything else is destroyed — prevent_destroy silently excludes that resource while allowing the rest of the destroy to proceed.
Terraform exits with an error and refuses to destroy anything — prevent_destroy causes the destroy plan to fail, blocking the entire operation.
The RDS instance is destroyed anyway because terraform destroy overrides lifecycle rules — the explicit destroy command takes precedence over lifecycle blocks.
The RDS instance is destroyed but Terraform creates an automatic snapshot first — prevent_destroy triggers a backup workflow before permitting deletion.
