questions.explanation: prevent_destroy causes Terraform to produce an error during planning if the resource would be destroyed. This halts the entire destroy operation, protecting the resource and blocking the plan.

questions.question: A developer hardcodes an API key in a Jenkinsfile so the pipeline can deploy to production. What is the biggest risk?

options.text: The pipeline will run slower because it has to parse the key inline — embedding secrets adds overhead to pipeline execution.
Anyone with repo read access can see the secret in version history — secrets in code are exposed to all contributors and persist in git history even after removal.
The API key will expire faster when stored in plain text — keys degrade quicker outside a vault due to lack of renewal mechanisms.
Jenkins will refuse to run the pipeline — most CI systems block pipelines that contain raw secrets as a built-in security measure.

questions.explanation: Hardcoded secrets in source control are visible to anyone with repository access and remain in git history permanently, making secret rotation and access control impossible.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline builds a Docker image using `FROM ubuntu:latest`. A week later the same commit produces a different image. What caused this?

options.text: Docker caches are corrupted on the build agent — stale layer caches cause images to diverge from expected output.
The `latest` tag is mutable and may point to a newer Ubuntu release — mutable tags resolve to different images over time, breaking reproducibility.
The Dockerfile syntax was deprecated between builds — Docker periodically updates its build parser which changes how instructions are interpreted.
The CI runner's CPU architecture changed — different architectures compile binaries differently, altering the final image hash.

questions.explanation: The `latest` tag is mutable and can be updated by the upstream maintainer at any time. Pinning to a specific digest or version tag ensures reproducible builds.

questions.question: Your team adopts ArgoCD and a developer manually runs `kubectl apply` to hotfix a production pod. Minutes later the change disappears. Why?

options.text: Kubernetes automatically rolls back any manual changes after a cooldown period — the control plane enforces immutability on running pods.
ArgoCD detected drift between the live state and the Git-declared state and reconciled it — GitOps controllers continuously sync the cluster to match the repository.
The pod's liveness probe failed after the hotfix and Kubernetes restarted it with the original spec — health checks override in-place changes.
The developer's kubeconfig lacked write permissions so the change was only applied in a dry-run — RBAC silently downgrades apply commands to dry-run.

questions.explanation: ArgoCD continuously reconciles live cluster state against the desired state in Git. Any manual changes that create drift are automatically reverted to match the Git source of truth.

questions.question: You need zero-downtime deploys with instant rollback for a stateless API. Traffic currently goes through an ALB. Which strategy fits best?

options.text: Rolling update with maxUnavailable=0 — this ensures old pods stay up until new ones pass health checks, preventing downtime during rollout.
Blue-green deployment — run two identical environments behind the ALB and switch traffic atomically, enabling instant rollback by reverting the target group.
Recreate deployment — tear down all old pods first and then bring up new ones, which is simplest for stateless services.
Canary with 1% traffic — route a tiny slice to the new version and gradually increase, which is ideal when instant rollback is the top priority.

questions.explanation: Blue-green deployments provide instant rollback by keeping the old environment running and switching the load balancer target group back in seconds. Canary is great for risk reduction but rollback is not as instantaneous.

questions.question: After deploying v2.3, error rates spike. You run `kubectl rollout undo deployment/api`. The errors persist. What is the most likely reason?

options.text: The rollback reuses the old ReplicaSet but the underlying node pool was scaled down — fewer nodes means pods stay in Pending state.
The issue is not in the application code but in a database migration that v2.3 applied — rollout undo reverts the pod spec but cannot reverse schema changes.
Kubernetes rate-limits rollbacks to one per minute by default — the undo command was queued and has not executed yet.
The rollback succeeded but the old image was garbage collected from the container registry — pods crash-loop pulling a deleted image.

questions.explanation: kubectl rollout undo only reverts the Deployment spec to the previous ReplicaSet. It cannot undo side effects like database schema migrations, data changes, or external state modifications.

questions.question: Your GitHub Actions workflow uses `actions/checkout@main` for a third-party action. A security auditor flags this. What is the concern?

options.text: The `main` branch reference can be updated by the action maintainer at any time — a compromised or changed action could exfiltrate secrets from your workflow without your knowledge.
GitHub rate-limits actions referenced by branch name — using a tag or SHA avoids API throttling during high-frequency CI runs.
Branch references bypass GitHub's action marketplace verification — only tagged releases are scanned for malware by GitHub.
Using `main` forces the workflow to always pull the latest runner image — this increases build time and may introduce OS-level incompatibilities.

questions.explanation: Referencing a mutable branch means the action code can change without your knowledge. Pinning to a specific commit SHA ensures you run exactly the code you reviewed.

questions.question: Your ArgoCD app-of-apps pattern manages 40 microservices. After a Git push updating all 40 manifests, ArgoCD reconciliation overwhelms the Kubernetes API server. How do you fix this?

options.text: Switch from ArgoCD to Flux because Flux batches all manifest applies into a single API call — this reduces API server load by design.
Configure sync waves and resource hooks to stagger deployments — this serializes reconciliation so only a subset of services sync at a time, reducing API server pressure.
Increase the API server's --max-requests-inflight flag to handle more concurrent requests — this scales the API server to match ArgoCD's throughput needs.
Set each ArgoCD Application to manual sync so all 40 must be triggered individually — this prevents automated reconciliation storms entirely.

questions.explanation: Sync waves let you control the order and parallelism of ArgoCD reconciliation. By assigning different wave numbers, you stagger syncs and avoid overwhelming the API server while keeping automation intact.

questions.question: Your application logs free-form text messages like `User 12345 logged in at 10:32`. An engineer proposes switching to structured JSON logs. What is the primary benefit?

options.text: JSON logs are smaller in size than plain text — structured formats use compression-friendly patterns that reduce storage costs.
Structured logs can be parsed, filtered, and queried by field without fragile regex — log aggregation tools can index JSON keys for fast, reliable searches.
JSON logs are encrypted by default — the structured format includes built-in field-level encryption for sensitive data.
Structured logs automatically include stack traces — JSON loggers capture the full call stack on every log entry for debugging.

questions.explanation: Structured JSON logs allow log aggregation systems to parse, index, and query by specific fields without brittle regular expressions, making troubleshooting faster and more reliable.

questions.question: Your team receives 200+ alerts per day and engineers start ignoring them. What is the most effective first step to address this?

options.text: Hire more on-call engineers to share the alert load — distributing alerts across a larger team reduces individual fatigue.
Review and remove alerts that have never led to action — eliminating non-actionable alerts reduces noise so engineers focus on real incidents.
Route all alerts to a shared Slack channel instead of paging — this reduces interruptions while still keeping visibility.
Increase alert thresholds across the board by 50% — higher thresholds mean fewer alerts fire and less noise overall.

questions.explanation: Alert fatigue is best addressed by auditing existing alerts and removing those that are non-actionable. Each remaining alert should require a specific human response.

questions.question: You are building a Grafana dashboard for an API service. Which set of metrics best represents the 'golden signals'?

options.text: CPU usage, memory usage, disk I/O, and network bandwidth — infrastructure metrics give a complete picture of service health.
Latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — these four signals directly measure user-facing behavior and resource pressure from the application's perspective.
Uptime percentage, deployment frequency, lead time, and MTTR — these DORA metrics capture both reliability and delivery performance.
Request count, pod restarts, log volume, and alert count — these operational metrics show how actively the system is behaving and recovering.

questions.explanation: The four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — were defined by Google's SRE book and provide the most effective view of a service's health from the user's perspective.

questions.question: A request to your checkout API is slow but the API's own metrics show low latency. The API calls three downstream services. How do you identify the bottleneck?

options.text: Add more logging to the checkout API — detailed logs at each step will reveal which internal function is slow.
Use distributed tracing to visualize the full request path across services — a trace shows span durations for each downstream call, pinpointing which service adds latency.
Check CPU and memory metrics on all three downstream services — the slow service will show resource saturation in its system metrics.
Increase the checkout API's timeout and retry limits — the slowness is likely caused by premature timeouts cutting off downstream responses.

questions.explanation: Distributed tracing propagates a trace ID across service boundaries, creating a timeline of spans for each hop. This directly reveals which downstream service or network segment contributes the most latency.

questions.question: Your APM tool shows p99 latency is 2s but p50 is 40ms for the same endpoint. What does this indicate?

options.text: The APM agent is misconfigured and sampling too few requests — low sample rates cause statistical outliers that inflate p99.
A small subset of requests hits a slow code path or external dependency — most requests are fast but roughly 1% encounter something like a cache miss or cold DB connection that spikes latency.
The server is under heavy load and all requests are slow — the p50 appears low because the APM tool averages out the fastest half.
The endpoint has a memory leak that accumulates over time — early requests are fast (p50) but later requests slow down as memory fills (p99).

questions.explanation: A large gap between p50 and p99 means most requests are fast but a small percentage hits a slow path — commonly caused by cache misses, cold connections, garbage collection pauses, or slow downstream calls.

questions.question: Your ELK stack ingests logs from 50 microservices. Search queries are increasingly slow. An engineer suggests the issue is high cardinality in a log field. Which field is the most likely culprit?

options.text: Log level (INFO, WARN, ERROR) — filtering by log level is the most common query pattern, so this field's index gets the heaviest read load.
A request ID that is unique per HTTP request — indexing a field with millions of unique values creates massive inverted indices that slow Elasticsearch queries.
Service name — having 50 distinct service names means Elasticsearch must maintain 50 index shards, one per service.
Timestamp — every log entry has a unique timestamp, and time-based indexing requires constant rebalancing of shards.

questions.explanation: High cardinality fields like unique request IDs create enormous inverted indices in Elasticsearch. While useful for correlation, indexing fields with millions of unique values significantly degrades query performance.

questions.question: Your SLO is 99.9% availability. You want an alert that fires only when the error budget burn rate threatens the monthly budget. Which approach is most effective?

options.text: Alert when error rate exceeds 0.1% for 5 minutes — this directly maps to the SLO threshold and catches any violation immediately.
Use a multi-window burn rate alert (e.g., 14.4x burn rate over 1 hour AND 6x over 6 hours) — this detects sustained error spikes that genuinely threaten the monthly budget while ignoring brief blips.
Alert when cumulative monthly errors exceed 50% of the budget — this guarantees you only page when half the budget is consumed, leaving room to respond.
Alert on a 30-day rolling error rate exceeding 0.1% — a long window smooths out noise and only fires when the overall trend is bad.

questions.explanation: Multi-window burn rate alerts, as described in Google's SRE workbook, combine a fast window (catching rapid burns) with a slow window (confirming sustained impact). This balances responsiveness with noise reduction.

questions.question: Your services use OpenTelemetry with head-based sampling at 10%. A rare payment failure (0.01% of traffic) is nearly impossible to trace. How do you capture these traces without storing all traffic?

options.text: Increase head-based sampling to 100% for the payment service only — this ensures every payment request is traced regardless of outcome.
Implement tail-based sampling at the collector — the collector sees completed traces and can keep those with errors or high latency, capturing rare failures without storing all traffic.
Add a custom span attribute for payment failures and filter in the tracing UI — this makes failures searchable even if the trace was not sampled.
Switch from OpenTelemetry to Jaeger's adaptive sampling — Jaeger automatically detects rare events and increases their sampling rate in real time.

questions.explanation: Tail-based sampling makes the keep/drop decision after the trace is complete, so it can selectively retain traces with errors, high latency, or specific attributes, capturing rare failures without the cost of 100% sampling.

questions.question: Your web application is hit by a SQL injection attack through a crafted URL parameter. Which layer of defense would have blocked this specific attack?

options.text: A network firewall blocking unused ports — firewalls filter traffic by port and IP, which stops unauthorized access but not malicious payloads on allowed ports.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) inspecting HTTP request content — WAFs analyze request bodies and parameters for known attack patterns like SQL injection signatures.
Enabling HTTPS with TLS 1.3 — encrypting traffic prevents attackers from reading or injecting payloads in transit.
Implementing IP allowlisting — restricting access to known IPs prevents unknown attackers from reaching the application at all.

questions.explanation: WAFs operate at Layer 7 and inspect HTTP request content for attack patterns like SQL injection. Network firewalls and TLS encrypt or filter at lower layers but do not inspect application-level payloads.

questions.question: A container image scan reveals a critical CVE in the base image's OpenSSL library. The application does not directly use OpenSSL. Should you fix it?

options.text: No — if the application does not use OpenSSL directly, the vulnerability is not exploitable and can be safely ignored.
Yes — other tools or libraries in the image may depend on OpenSSL transitively, and an attacker could exploit it through those indirect paths.
No — CVEs in base images are the upstream maintainer's responsibility and will be patched in the next release automatically.
Yes, but only if the container runs as root — non-root containers cannot be exploited through library vulnerabilities.

questions.explanation: Transitive dependencies and system utilities in the image may use OpenSSL. A critical CVE should be addressed by updating the base image or library, regardless of direct application usage.

questions.question: A new developer needs to SSH into a production server. The team currently uses shared passwords. What should you implement instead?

options.text: Individual SSH key pairs per developer — key-based auth is stronger than passwords, enables per-user access control, and keys can be revoked individually when someone leaves.
A stronger shared password rotated monthly — regular rotation limits exposure time if the password leaks and is easier to manage than individual keys.
VPN-only access with the same shared password — adding a VPN layer makes the password irrelevant since attackers cannot reach the server.
IP allowlisting for each developer's home IP — restricting by IP makes password strength irrelevant since only trusted locations can connect.

questions.explanation: Individual SSH key pairs provide strong authentication, per-user audit trails, and the ability to revoke access for a single user without affecting others. Shared credentials make accountability and offboarding impossible.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs container image scanning after pushing the image to the registry. A security engineer says this is too late. What should change?

options.text: Scan the image before pushing to the registry — failing the build on critical CVEs prevents vulnerable images from ever being stored or deployed.
Scan only at deploy time using an admission controller — this is the only point where you know the image will actually run in production.
Scan the Dockerfile source code instead of the built image — static analysis of the Dockerfile catches vulnerabilities earlier than image scanning.
Scan images nightly in the registry using a cron job — batch scanning is more efficient and catches newly disclosed CVEs.

questions.explanation: Scanning before registry push follows shift-left principles — vulnerabilities are caught in CI before the image is published, preventing vulnerable images from being available for deployment.

questions.question: Two internal microservices communicate over the private network. An architect recommends adding mTLS between them. A developer argues the private network is already secure. Who is right?

options.text: The developer — private networks are isolated by design and adding mTLS only adds latency with no meaningful security benefit.
The architect — mTLS provides mutual authentication and encryption, protecting against lateral movement if any host on the private network is compromised.
The developer — mTLS is only necessary for external-facing traffic where you cannot trust the network path.
The architect, but only if the services handle PII — mTLS is overkill for non-sensitive internal traffic and adds unnecessary operational complexity.

questions.explanation: Zero-trust principles assume the network can be compromised. mTLS ensures both services verify each other's identity and encrypts traffic, protecting against insider threats and lateral movement.

questions.question: Your Kubernetes cluster has a flat network — all pods can communicate with all other pods. How do you restrict the blast radius if one pod is compromised?

options.text: Deploy each service in a separate Kubernetes cluster — physical isolation is the only reliable way to prevent lateral movement between services.
Apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to allow only required pod-to-pod traffic — this creates micro-segmentation at the network level so a compromised pod cannot reach unrelated services.
Use Kubernetes RBAC to restrict pod service accounts — RBAC controls API server access, which prevents a compromised pod from making API calls to other workloads.
Set all pods to run as non-root — restricting container privileges prevents network-level exploits since non-root processes cannot open raw sockets.

questions.explanation: NetworkPolicies enforce micro-segmentation by specifying which pods can communicate. This limits lateral movement from a compromised pod to only its explicitly allowed destinations.

questions.question: Your container runs as root and mounts the Docker socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`). Why is this a critical security risk?

options.text: Mounting the Docker socket lets the container manage other containers on the host — an attacker could start a privileged container that escapes to the host, achieving full node compromise.
The Docker socket exposes container logs to other containers — sensitive data in logs becomes readable by any container on the same host.
The Docker socket allows the container to modify its own resource limits — an attacker could allocate unlimited CPU and memory, causing a denial-of-service on the host.
Mounting host sockets disables seccomp and AppArmor profiles for that container — the container loses all kernel-level sandboxing protections.

questions.explanation: The Docker socket grants full control of the Docker daemon. A compromised container can use it to launch a new privileged container with host filesystem access, effectively escaping the container boundary entirely.

questions.question: An EC2 instance running a web app has an IAM role with `s3:*` and `ec2:*` permissions. The app only reads from one S3 bucket. What is the risk and fix?

options.text: The broad permissions violate least privilege — if the instance is compromised, the attacker can access all S3 buckets and control EC2 instances. Scope the policy to `s3:GetObject` on the specific bucket ARN only.
IAM roles on EC2 are inherently insecure — use IAM users with access keys stored in environment variables for more granular control.
The wildcard permissions increase AWS API costs — each API call is billed, and broad permissions enable more calls. Restrict permissions to reduce billing.
The ec2:* permission allows the instance to terminate itself — this creates an availability risk. Remove ec2:* but keep s3:* since S3 is read-only by default.

questions.explanation: Overly broad IAM permissions violate least privilege. An SSRF or RCE exploit on the instance could leverage the instance profile to access all S3 data and control EC2 resources. Scope permissions to exactly what the app needs.

questions.question: Your team argues about whether to set an SLO of 99.99% or 99.9% for an internal admin dashboard used by 10 people. Which is more appropriate?

options.text: 99.99% — all production services should target four nines regardless of audience to maintain engineering standards.
99.9% — a low-traffic internal tool does not justify the engineering cost of four nines, and the extra budget allows faster iteration.
99.99% — internal users are less tolerant of downtime because they have no alternative tools and cannot retry later.
Neither — internal tools should not have SLOs because they are not customer-facing and do not affect revenue.

questions.explanation: SLOs should reflect the user impact and business value. An internal dashboard for 10 users does not warrant the engineering investment of 99.99%. A 99.9% SLO provides reliability while leaving room for faster development.

questions.question: Your service has a 99.9% monthly SLO. It is day 15 and you have consumed 80% of the error budget. What should you do?

options.text: Continue shipping features normally — the budget exists to be spent and 80% used at the halfway point means you still have 20% remaining.
Freeze feature releases and focus on reliability work — at this burn rate you will exhaust the budget before month-end, so prioritize stability to protect the SLO.
Lower the SLO to 99.5% for the rest of the month — adjusting the target mid-cycle avoids a breach and resets the budget.
Increase server capacity to reduce error rates — more infrastructure headroom will naturally bring errors down and preserve the remaining budget.

questions.explanation: Consuming 80% of the error budget halfway through the month signals reliability risk. The standard SRE response is to slow feature velocity and focus on reliability to avoid breaching the SLO.

questions.question: After a major outage, management wants to identify which engineer caused the failure. What SRE practice should you advocate for?

options.text: A blameless postmortem focused on systemic causes — blaming individuals discourages reporting and hides process failures that are the real root cause.
A private review with the responsible engineer and their manager — keeping accountability confidential protects the individual while still addressing the mistake.
An automated root cause analysis tool — removing human judgment from postmortems ensures objectivity and prevents blame.
Skip the postmortem if the fix is already deployed — the issue is resolved and rehashing it wastes team time that could go toward new features.

questions.explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on what systemic factors allowed the failure, not who caused it. This culture encourages transparency, surfaces process gaps, and leads to durable fixes.

questions.question: Your service handles 10k RPS at 60% CPU. Marketing announces a campaign expected to triple traffic next month. How do you capacity plan?

options.text: Triple the current instance count to match the traffic increase — linear scaling ensures 1:1 capacity per request regardless of efficiency.

Load test at 30k RPS to identify actual bottlenecks, then scale based on the results — real load testing reveals non-linear bottlenecks like database connections and memory that may require different scaling strategies.
Enable autoscaling and let it handle the traffic increase dynamically — autoscalers react to real-time demand so no advance planning is needed.
Add a CDN in front of the service — caching will absorb most of the traffic increase without requiring any backend scaling.

questions.explanation: Traffic rarely scales linearly with resources. Load testing at the expected volume reveals actual bottlenecks like database limits, connection pools, or memory, enabling targeted capacity planning rather than blind scaling.

questions.question: An SRE spends 4 hours every Monday manually restarting a batch job that fails due to stale credentials. This has happened for 6 months. What is this an example of?

options.text: Incident response — the SRE is performing first-responder duties by restoring a failing job, which is a core SRE responsibility.
Toil — this is repetitive, automatable, manual work that scales linearly with the problem and provides no lasting value.
Technical debt — the stale credential issue is an architectural flaw that needs refactoring but is not the same as operational toil.
Operational overhead — some manual work is inherent to running production systems and cannot be eliminated.

questions.explanation: Toil is manual, repetitive, automatable work that provides no enduring value. Google's SRE book defines toil as work that scales linearly and should be automated away to free engineers for project work.

questions.question: You want to introduce chaos engineering to your production Kubernetes cluster. Your manager is concerned about customer impact. What is the safest first step?

options.text: Run chaos experiments in staging first to validate hypotheses — staging environments let you observe failure modes without risking production customer impact.
Start in production by killing a single non-critical pod during low traffic — chaos engineering is only valid in production, and starting small limits blast radius.
Simulate failures using feature flags that return error responses — this tests application resilience without actually disrupting infrastructure.
Wait until the system has 99.99% availability for three months before introducing any chaos — you need a reliability baseline before you can measure chaos impact.

questions.explanation: Starting chaos experiments in a staging environment builds confidence and surfaces obvious issues before production. Once the team gains experience, experiments can be carefully extended to production with blast radius controls.

questions.question: Your e-commerce checkout API has an SLO of 99.95% success rate. The SLI is currently measured as HTTP 2xx responses divided by total responses. A teammate points out this misses slow-but-successful requests. What should you add?

options.text: A latency SLI that counts requests completing under a threshold (e.g., 500ms) as good — users who wait too long may abandon checkout even if the response succeeds.
A throughput SLI measuring requests per second — low throughput indicates the system is rejecting requests, which the success rate alone does not capture.
An availability SLI based on health check endpoints — synthetic health checks catch outages faster than real-user metrics.
A saturation SLI measuring CPU and memory utilization — high saturation predicts future failures even when current requests succeed.

questions.explanation: A latency SLI captures user experience degradation that a success rate SLI misses. For checkout flows, a slow-but-successful request may still cause cart abandonment, making latency a critical quality indicator.

questions.question: During a P1 incident, the team discovers three possible causes. The incident commander sees engineers investigating all three in parallel without coordination. What should the IC do?

options.text: Let them continue — parallel investigation is the fastest way to find the root cause and the IC should not slow things down.

Assign each cause to a specific person or pair, set check-in intervals, and have them report findings back — structured parallel investigation prevents duplicated effort and keeps the IC informed for decision-making.
Stop all parallel investigation and focus the entire team on the most likely cause — serial investigation ensures no effort is wasted on unlikely theories.
Escalate to senior management for additional staffing — a P1 with three possible causes needs more engineers, not better coordination.

questions.explanation: Effective incident management uses structured parallel investigation — the IC assigns ownership, prevents duplication, and collects findings at regular intervals to make informed decisions about next steps.

questions.question: Your service has been at 100% availability for three months with plenty of error budget remaining. Product management wants to push an aggressive, risky feature. What is the SRE perspective?

options.text: Block the release — 100% availability means the current system is perfectly reliable and any risk threatens that track record.
Approve the release — unused error budget means you can afford the risk, and the budget exists precisely to enable innovation velocity while protecting the SLO.
Tighten the SLO to 99.99% since the team has proven it can achieve 100% — a harder target better reflects actual capabilities.
Delay the release until next quarter and bank the unused budget — carrying over budget provides a larger safety net for future high-risk changes.

questions.explanation: Error budgets exist to balance reliability with feature velocity. Unused budget signals that the team can take more risk. Blocking releases when budget is available over-prioritizes stability at the cost of product progress.

questions.question: Your AWS bill doubled last month but no new services were launched. What should you check first?

options.text: Check if AWS raised their pricing tier — cloud providers periodically adjust prices which can cause unexpected bill increases.

Review Cost Explorer for the specific service and region showing the spike — identifying which line item grew tells you exactly where to investigate, such as an orphaned resource or unexpected data transfer.
Contact AWS support to dispute the charges — sudden spikes are often billing errors that AWS will credit back.
Immediately delete all non-production resources — cutting development and staging environments is the fastest way to reduce costs.

questions.explanation: Cost Explorer breaks down spending by service, region, and usage type. Identifying the specific cost driver is essential before taking action — common causes include orphaned resources, data transfer, or autoscaling events.

questions.question: CloudWatch shows your m5.2xlarge instances consistently use only 10% CPU and 15% memory. What is the most appropriate action?

options.text: Keep the current size for headroom — low utilization provides a buffer for unexpected traffic spikes and prevents performance issues.
Rightsize to a smaller instance type like m5.large — consistently low utilization indicates over-provisioning, and downsizing saves cost while still leaving adequate headroom.
Switch to burstable t3 instances — burstable instances are always cheaper and automatically handle spikes with CPU credits.
Add more instances behind a load balancer — distributing load across more small instances is always more cost-effective than a single large instance.

questions.explanation: Consistent low utilization is a clear signal of over-provisioning. Rightsizing to a smaller instance type reduces cost while maintaining sufficient capacity. Always validate with load testing after resizing.

questions.question: Multiple teams share one AWS account. Finance cannot determine which team owns each cost. What governance practice should you implement first?

options.text: Separate each team into its own AWS account — account-level isolation is the only reliable way to attribute costs.
Implement a mandatory tagging policy with team, environment, and project tags — cost allocation tags let you group and filter costs by owner in Cost Explorer without restructuring accounts.
Have each team submit monthly expense reports estimating their usage — manual tracking creates accountability and costs nothing to implement.
Use AWS Budgets to set a spending cap per team — budgets automatically restrict spending so finance does not need per-team cost attribution.

questions.explanation: A mandatory tagging strategy is the fastest way to enable cost allocation by team. AWS Cost Explorer can group costs by tag, providing instant visibility without the overhead of restructuring accounts.

questions.question: Your workload runs 24/7 on on-demand EC2 instances and has been stable for 12 months. You want to reduce costs by 30-40%. Which purchasing option is most appropriate?

options.text: Spot instances — they offer up to 90% savings and work well for any long-running workload since interruptions are rare in practice.
Reserved Instances or Savings Plans with a 1-year commitment — a stable 24/7 workload is the ideal use case for commitments, delivering 30-40% savings with no interruption risk.
Scheduled Reserved Instances for business hours only — since most traffic occurs during the day, paying for 24/7 capacity is wasteful.
Switch to Lambda to pay only per invocation — serverless eliminates idle costs and always saves money compared to always-on instances.

questions.explanation: Stable, predictable 24/7 workloads are the ideal candidate for Reserved Instances or Savings Plans. A 1-year commitment provides 30-40% savings with no risk of interruption, unlike Spot instances.

questions.question: Your CFO asks why cloud costs increased 20% this quarter. You show a single total-spend graph. The CFO is not satisfied. What is missing from your report?

options.text: A breakdown by service, team, and environment with month-over-month trends — granular attribution shows exactly where growth occurred and whether it aligns with business growth.
A comparison with competitor cloud pricing — showing that your provider is cheaper than alternatives justifies the total spend increase.
The number of support tickets filed with the cloud provider — this proves the team is actively optimizing and not ignoring cost issues.
A forecast showing costs will decrease next quarter — projecting future savings reassures the CFO that the current increase is temporary.

questions.explanation: Executives need cost attribution — where is money being spent, by whom, and why. Granular breakdowns by service, team, and environment with trends enable informed decisions about where to optimize.

questions.question: Your team stores 50TB of application logs in S3 Standard. Logs older than 30 days are rarely accessed. How do you reduce storage costs?

options.text: Delete all logs older than 30 days — if they are rarely accessed, they have no value and should be purged to save costs.
Compress the logs using gzip before uploading — compression reduces object sizes by 80-90%, which is more impactful than changing storage classes.

Create an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition logs older than 30 days to S3 Glacier or Infrequent Access — tiered storage dramatically reduces costs for rarely accessed data while keeping it retrievable.
Move all logs to EBS volumes — block storage is cheaper per GB than S3 for large datasets and provides faster read access.

questions.explanation: S3 Lifecycle policies automatically transition objects to cheaper storage tiers like Infrequent Access or Glacier based on age. This can reduce storage costs by 40-80% for data that is still needed but rarely read.

questions.question: A developer launches a p3.16xlarge GPU instance for a one-hour ML experiment and forgets to terminate it. The instance runs for 3 weeks costing $15,000. How do you prevent this?

options.text: Block all GPU instance types via IAM policy — preventing access entirely eliminates the risk of forgotten expensive instances.

Implement AWS Budgets with alerts AND enforce auto-termination tags or Lambda functions that shut down untagged or expired resources — cost alerts catch overruns while automated cleanup prevents orphaned resources.
Require manager approval for all AWS console logins — adding a human gate ensures oversight over every resource action.
Switch to preemptible instances for all ML workloads — they auto-terminate after a set period, solving the orphan problem entirely.

questions.explanation: Combining budget alerts with automated resource cleanup (via tags with expiration dates and Lambda-based enforcement) catches both the cost anomaly and prevents orphaned resources without blocking legitimate use.

questions.question: Your Kubernetes cluster runs mixed workloads: a stable API (70% of compute) and batch ML jobs (30%). You have a 1-year RI covering 70% of cluster capacity. How do you optimize the remaining 30%?

options.text: Buy another RI covering the remaining 30% — full RI coverage gives maximum savings and the batch jobs run frequently enough to justify it.

Use Spot instances for the batch ML workloads with checkpointing — batch jobs are fault-tolerant by nature and can resume from checkpoints, making Spot's 60-90% discount ideal for this interruptible workload.
Migrate batch jobs to Lambda — serverless eliminates idle compute costs and scales automatically with job queue depth.
Run batch jobs only during off-peak hours on the RI capacity — the stable API uses less compute at night, freeing RI capacity for batch processing.

questions.explanation: Batch ML workloads are fault-tolerant and can handle interruptions with checkpointing, making them ideal for Spot instances. The 60-90% discount on Spot significantly reduces the cost of the variable 30% of compute.

questions.question: Three product teams share a Kubernetes cluster. The platform team wants to implement chargeback but containers share nodes and networking. What is the most accurate approach?

options.text: Split cluster costs equally among the three teams — equal allocation is fair when teams share infrastructure and avoids the complexity of measuring actual usage.

Allocate costs based on namespace resource requests and actual usage using a tool like Kubecost — tracking CPU, memory, and storage per namespace provides accurate per-team attribution including shared costs like cluster overhead.
Give each team a dedicated node pool and charge based on node pool costs — physical isolation makes cost attribution trivial since each team pays for their own nodes.
Charge based on the number of pods each team runs — pod count is a simple and objective metric that correlates well with resource consumption.

questions.explanation: Tools like Kubecost track actual CPU, memory, and storage consumption per namespace and can allocate shared costs like control plane and networking proportionally, providing accurate chargeback in multi-tenant clusters.
